workflow: Set review_decision to accepted or rejected.
Accepted entries must complete the curation block before fixture build.
Reviewed files can be passed to build_manual_fixture_from_review_candidates.py.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Bus Terminal Ventilation Concern
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Primary concerns in enclosed bus loading areas are health and safety problems requiring proper ventilation.

candidate_entries.evidence_text:
Licensed for single user. © 2019 ASHRAE, Inc.3.8 2019 ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC Applications (SI)
Cruise Terminals. Ventilation design must ensure that fumes
and odors from forklifts and cargo in work areas do not penetrate
occupied and administrative areas.
Bus Terminals. The primary concerns with enclosed bus loading
areas are health and safety probl ems, which must be handled by
proper ventilation (see Chapter 16). Although diesel  engine fumes
are generally not as noxious as ga soline fumes, bus terminals often
have many buses loading and unloading at the same time, and thetotal amount of fumes and odors may be disturbing.
In terms of health and safety, enclosed bus loading areas and
automobile parking garages pres ent the most serious problems.
Three major problems are encountered , the first and most serious of
which is emission of carbon monoxide (CO) by cars and oxides of
nitrogen (NO
x) by buses, which can caus e serious illness and pos-
sibly death.
candidate_entries.judge_reason: Evidence clearly describes primary health and safety concerns in bus terminals requiring proper ventilation.

curation.summary: Draft review summary for application_guidance hvac:chiller:application_guidance:bus_terminal_ventilation_concern derived from chunk chunk_72d152e80c574931 on page 1 for Chiller.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Underground Garage CO Monitoring
structured_payload_candidate.summary: All underground garages should have facilities for testing CO concentration or periodic checks.

candidate_entries.evidence_text:
Oil and gasoline fu mes, which may cause nausea and
headaches and can create a fire haza rd, are also of concern. The third
issue is lack of air movement and the resulting stale atmosphere
caused by increased CO content in  the air. This condition may cause
headaches or grogginess. Most codes require a min imum ventilation
rate to ensure that the CO concen tration does not exceed safe limits.
Chapter 16 covers ventilation requirements and calculation proce-
dures for enclosed vehicula r facilities in detail.
All underground garages should have facilities for testing the CO
concentration or should have the garage checked periodically. Prob-
lems such as clogged duct syst ems; improperly operating fans,
motors, or dampers; or clogged ai r intake or exha ust louvers may
not allow proper air circulation.  Proper maintenance is required to
minimize any operational defects.
3.
candidate_entries.judge_reason: Evidence explicitly recommends CO monitoring or periodic checks in underground garages; operational guidance.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Perchloric Acid Hood Duct Material
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Ducts for perchloric acid exhaust systems should be constructed of 316L stainless steel or suitable nonmetallic material.

candidate_entries.evidence_text:
Ducts should be c onstructed of a stainless steel
material, with a chromium and nickel content not less than that of
316L stainless steel, or of a suit able nonmetallic material. Joints
should be welded and ground smooth . A perchloric acid exhaust
system should only be used for work involving perchloric acid.
Application : Process and research la boratories using perchloric
acid. Mandatory use becaus e of explosion hazard.
California.  Special hood with sash openi ngs on multiple sides (u su-
ally horizontal).
Applic ation : For enclosing large and complex research apparatus
that require access from two or more sides.
Floor-Mounted Hood (Walk-In).  Hood with sash openings to the
floor. Sash can be either horizontal or vertical.
Application : For enclosing large or complex research apparatus.
Not designed for personnel to enter while operations are in prog-
ress.
Distillation.
candidate_entries.judge_reason: Evidence specifies required duct material (316L stainless steel or nonmetallic) for perchloric acid exhaust.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Perchloric Acid Hood Washdown
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Exhaust system for perchloric acid hoods must have internal water washdown and drainage system.

candidate_entries.evidence_text:
Hood with special integr al work surfaces, coved
corners, and nonorganic lining ma terials. Perchloric acid is anextremely active oxidizing agent.  Its vapors can form unstable
deposits in the ductwork that present a potential explosion haz-
ard. To alleviate this hazard, the exhaust system must beequipped with an internal water washdown and drainage system,
and the ductwork must be constructed of smooth, impervious,cleanable materials that are resistant to acid attack. The internal
washdown system must completely flush the ductwork, exhaust
fan, discharge stack, and fume  hood inner surfaces. Ductwork
should be kept as short as poss ible with minimum elbows. Per-
chloric acid exhaust systems wi th longer duct runs may need a
zoned washdown system to avoid water flow rates in excess of
the capacity to drain water from the hood. Because perchloric
acid is an extremely active oxidizing agent, organic materialsshould not be used in the exhaust system in places such as joints
and gaskets.
candidate_entries.judge_reason: Evidence mandates internal water washdown and drainage system for perchloric acid hoods.

curation.title: Draft Application Guidance hvac:chiller:application_guidance:perchloric_acid_hood_washdown for Chiller
curation.summary: Draft review summary for application_guidance hvac:chiller:application_guidance:perchloric_acid_hood_washdown derived from chunk chunk_7b4f2ad6d9f8401b on page 2 for Chiller.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Fume Hood Bypass Air Prevention
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Fume hoods with vertically rising sash sections should include provisions to prevent bypass of ceiling plenum air.

candidate_entries.evidence_text:
A horiz ontally operable
sash incorporates multiple panels that slide in multiple tracks,
allowing the open area to be positioned across the face of the hood.
The combination of a horizontally operable sash m ounted within a
single vertically operabl e sash section allows the entire hood face to
be opened for setup. The opening area  can then be limited by closing
the vertical panel, with only the horizontally sliding sash sections
used during experimentati on. Both the multiple vertical sash section
and combination sash arrangement allow the use of larger fumehoods with limited openi ng areas, resulting in reduced exhaust air-
flow requirements. Fume  hoods with vertically rising sash sections
should include provisions around the sash to prevent the bypass of
ceiling plenum air into the fume hood. Fig. 1 Bypass Fume Hood  with Vertical Sash
and Bypass Air Inlet
This file is licensed to Osama Khayata (osama@ashraeuae.org). Copyright ASHRAE 2019.
Telegram EDUFIRE_IR
EDUFIRE.IR.
candidate_entries.judge_reason: Evidence advises including provisions to prevent bypass of ceiling plenum air in fume hoods with vertical sash.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Cultural Heritage Latent Capacity
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Adequate latent capacity is critical in cultural heritage applications; often disregarded with two-pipe and standard package unit designs.

candidate_entries.evidence_text:
In humid climates, special attention should be
given to the sensible and latent lo ads’ variations for cases where the
warehouse or distribution center is  conditioned or cooled by ther-
mostatically controlled packaged HVAC equipment. In these cli-mates, it is common to satisfy the space temperature (i.e., very low
or no sensible cooling load), but, because of infiltration of moist air
and without proper cooling (i.e., th e cooling equipment is off), for
space humidity to be  unacceptably high.
This file is licensed to Osama Khayata (osama@ashraeuae.org). Copyright ASHRAE 2019.
Telegram EDUFIRE_IR
EDUFIRE.IR.
candidate_entries.judge_reason: Evidence highlights need for adequate latent capacity in conditioned warehouses; operational concern supported.
